sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 26, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Pink bar is a timer/step counter (shrinking). Player stuck below chamber at cols 16-19. Projectile stays in chamber. Player might be able to move UP outside the chamber's column range.
- Plan: Move RIGHT to exit chamber column (cols 16-19), then try UP to move freely above. Then navigate to targets.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 25 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so even outside the chamber column, the player can't move UP. The player is at rows 36-39, cols 24-26, and UP didn't work.
Wait, the player's rows changed from 37-39 to 36-39. That's interesting - the player expanded upward by 1 row! But the player didn't actually move up, just expanded.
Actually wait, let me re-read. Before the RIGHT moves, the player was at rows 37-39, cols 16-19. After RIGHT, RIGHT, the player is at rows 36-39, cols 24-26. So the player's row range changed from 37-39 to 36-39.
Hmm, but the player has 12 pixels. If it's at rows 36-39 (4 rows) and cols 24-26 (3 cols), that's 4*3 = 12 cells. So the player is a 4x3 rectangle.
Wait, but earlier the player was at rows 37-39 (3 rows) and cols 16-19 (4 cols), which is also 3*4 = 12 cells. So the player is a 3x4 rectangle when in the chamber column, but becomes a 4x3 rectangle when outside?
That's weird. Let me check the ascii to see the player's shape.
Actually, maybe the player's shape changes based on position. Or maybe I'm misreading the segmentation.
Let me focus on the main issue: the player can't move UP anywhere on the board. This is a fundamental constraint.
But wait, the player destroyed target C earlier by pressing SPACE. And target C was at rows 36-39, cols 32-35. The player was at rows 37-39, cols 16-19 (or similar). So the player didn't need to be adjacent to target C to destroy it - SPACE worked from a distance.
So maybe SPACE is a ranged attack. When the player presses SPACE, it shoots a projectile in the direction the player is facing. But the player doesn't have a facing direction...
Or maybe SPACE shoots the projectile upward from the chamber. When the player is aligned with a target (same column range), the projectile hits that target.
But the projectile is in the chamber at cols 16-19, which is aligned with target B. So pressing SPACE should shoot the projectile at target B. But it doesn't - it just toggles the chamber state.
Wait, maybe I need the chamber to be in a specific state (dark gray) for the projectile to launch. Let me try:
1. Press SPACE to toggle chamber to dark gray
2. Press SPACE again - maybe this launches the projectile when the chamber is active
Actually, I already tried this. The chamber just toggles between white and dark gray.
Let me think about this differently. What if the game is not about destroying targets with projectiles? What if the game is about something else entirely?
Looking at the board again:
- Two target frames (A and B) with blue centers
- A horizontal bar with gray inner part
- A chamber with a blue projectile
- A player
- A pink bar (timer)
What if the goal is to collect all blue centers? The player needs to "collect" the blue centers from the targets and the chamber.
But how? The player can't move UP to reach the targets.
What if SPACE is not a shooting action but a "collect" action? When the player presses SPACE while aligned with a target, it collects the blue center from that target.
But I already tried SPACE when aligned with target B, and it just toggled the chamber.
Hmm, let me reconsider. When I destroyed target C, the player was aligned with target C (same column range). The player pressed SPACE, and target C's frame disappeared, leaving just the blue center. Then the blue center appeared in the chamber.
So the sequence was:
1. Player aligned with target C
2. SPACE → target C destroyed, blue center falls into chamber
3. Now player aligned with target B
4. SPACE → chamber toggles (doesn't destroy target B)
The difference is that target C was at rows 36-39 (same row range as the player), while target B is at rows 28-31 (above the player). Maybe SPACE only works on targets that are at the same row level as the player?
But target A is at rows 24-27, which is even higher. So SPACE wouldn't work on target A either.
Wait, but target C was destroyed. How was the player aligned with target C? Let me check the history.
Looking at the history, the initial state had target C at rows 36-39, cols 32-35. The player was at rows 37-39, cols 16-19 (or similar). So target C and the player were at the same row level.
When the player moved RIGHT to align with target C (cols 32-35), SPACE destroyed target C. This makes sense - SPACE works on targets at the same row level.
So the mechanic is: SPACE destroys targets that are at the same row level as the player. Since target B is above the player, SPACE doesn't destroy it.
But then how do I destroy target B? I need to move the player UP to the same row level as target B.
But the player can't move UP! This is the core problem.
Wait, maybe there's a way to move the player UP. Let me think about what I haven't tried:
1. The player can move LEFT, RIGHT, DOWN, UP (but UP doesn't work)
2. SPACE toggles the chamber or destroys targets at the same level
3. The projectile is in the chamber
What if I need to destroy the projectile first? Or move the projectile out of the way?
Actually, let me re-examine the UP movement. The player is at rows 36-39. When I try UP, the player doesn't move. But maybe the player CAN move UP if there's no obstacle above.
The chamber is at rows 32-35, which is above the player. So the player can't move UP because the chamber is blocking.
But what if I move the player RIGHT, away from the chamber column, and then try UP? I already tried this, and the player still couldn't move UP.
Hmm, but the player expanded from rows 37-39 to 36-39 when moving RIGHT. Maybe the player is slowly moving UP, one row at a time, when moving horizontally?
Let me check: the player started at rows 37-39. After moving RIGHT twice, the player is at rows 36-39. So the player moved UP by 1 row.
Wait, but then I tried UP and the player didn't move. So the player is at rows 36-39 now.
Let me try moving RIGHT more and see if the player continues to move UP.
Actually, I think I need to reconsider the player's shape. The player has 12 pixels. If it's a 3x4 rectangle, it occupies 3 rows and 4 columns. But when the player moves RIGHT, the shape might change.
Let me check the ascii to see the player's current shape.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player can't move UP even outside chamber column. Player expanded from rows 37-39 to 36-39 when moving RIGHT - maybe horizontal movement gradually moves player upward?
Plan: Check player shape in ascii, then try more RIGHT movement to see if player continues rising.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check player shape in ascii
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Player area:")
for r in range(34, 42):
    row_str = ascii_lines[r]
    cols_slice = row_str[20:30] if len(row_str) > 29 else row_str
    print(f"Row {r}: '{cols_slice}'")

# Try moving RIGHT more to see if player rises
print("\nMoving RIGHT more...")
predict(expect_change=True, note='More RIGHT movement')
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player after 3 RIGHT: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check ascii
print("\nPlayer area after RIGHT:")
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
for r in range(34, 42):
    row_str = ascii_lines[r]
    cols_slice = row_str[26:36] if len(row_str) > 35 else row_str
    print(f"Row {r}: '{cols_slice}'")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
